title: D.B. Cooper: The Man Who Vanished From the Sky

segments.text: Some criminals get caught. Some quietly disappear.
But only one man committed the perfect crime in mid-air.
He jumped out of a passenger jet with a fortune in cash.
And was never seen again. No body. No name. No answers.
For more than fifty years, the world has asked the same question. Who was he?
This is the story of D.B. Cooper.
The early 1970s were a strange time for air travel.
Hijackings had become so common they were almost routine.
But none would become as legendary as what happened on November 24th, 1971.
It was the day before Thanksgiving, at the airport in Portland, Oregon.
A man in a dark suit and tie walked up to the counter.
He bought a one-way ticket to Seattle, and paid in cash.
He gave his name as Dan Cooper.
A reporting error would later twist that name into D.B. Cooper.
He boarded the Boeing 727 calmly, and took a seat near the back.
Witnesses said he was quiet, polite, and unremarkable.
A man you would forget the moment he passed you.
He ordered a drink, lit a cigarette, and waited for the plane to leave the ground.
Shortly after takeoff, he handed a flight attendant a note.
At first, she slipped it into her pocket without reading it.
She assumed it was just a lonely businessman's phone number.
But the man leaned closer and said something that froze the cabin.
Miss, you'd better look at that note. I have a bomb.
He opened his briefcase just enough to reveal wires and red cylinders.
Whether it was real or not, no one was willing to find out.
His demands were calm, and chillingly specific.
Two hundred thousand dollars in twenty dollar bills. And four parachutes.
The crew relayed the message to the ground, and authorities chose to cooperate.
While the money was gathered, the plane circled above Seattle.
The passengers had no idea anything was wrong.
After two hours, the jet finally touched down.
The ransom and the parachutes were brought on board.
True to his word, Cooper released all thirty six passengers.
But he kept several crew members on the plane with him.
Then he gave the pilots a new and unusual order.
Fly toward Mexico City. Slow, and low.
Keep the landing gear down, and the cabin unpressurized.
He knew exactly how to make the jump survivable.
Somewhere over the black forests of southern Washington, he made his move.
He sent the crew to the front and lowered the rear airstair himself.
Freezing wind and rain tore through the open back of the plane.
And then, at around eight in the evening, he stepped into the darkness.
Falling into the storm, the wind, and the trees below.
When the plane landed, the crew opened the cabin. The man was gone.
All he left behind was a clip-on tie, eight cigarette butts, and two of the parachutes.
And so began one of the largest manhunts in American history.
Agents and soldiers combed the wilderness for any sign of him.
They searched for weeks, in some of the harshest terrain in the country.
They found nothing. No parachute. No money. No body.
It was as if the man had simply dissolved into the night.
The FBI opened an investigation they code-named Norjak.
For years, every lead turned cold.
Then, in 1980, came the only real piece of evidence.
A young boy was digging on a riverbank along the Columbia River.
Buried in the sand, he found rotting bundles of twenty dollar bills.
Nearly six thousand dollars, decayed but still bound together.
The serial numbers matched Cooper's ransom exactly.
It remains the only part of the money ever recovered.
And it only deepened the mystery. How did it get there?
Over the decades, the FBI investigated more than a thousand suspects.
Some seemed to fit perfectly. A few even confessed on their deathbeds.
But not a single one was ever confirmed to be the man on that plane.
Each promising lead eventually collapsed under the evidence.
His legend grew far beyond the crime itself.
It seeped into music, film, and television.
In the show Prison Break, a character named Charles Westmoreland claims to be D.B. Cooper himself.
To many, he became a folk hero. The man who beat the system and walked away.
In 2016, after forty five years, the FBI finally closed the case.
Officially, it remains the only unsolved skyjacking in American history.
Did Cooper survive that jump into the freezing storm?
Or did the wilderness quietly swallow him forever?
Some criminals get caught. Some quietly disappear.
But D.B. Cooper stepped out of that plane, and straight into legend.
The only man who ever pulled it off. And was never found.
This was just one case the world never forgot. Subscribe. There are many more.
Subscribe for cases the world never forgot.

description: The complete story of D.B. Cooper, the only unsolved skyjacking in American history. Follow for more cold cases.
